source_paper_title: Efficient Approximation Schemes for Stochastic Probing and Selection-Stopping Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Segev_2025, title={Efficient Approximation Schemes for Stochastic Probing and Selection-Stopping Problems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0242}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0242}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Segev, Danny and Singla, Sahil}, year={2025}, month=dec }
context:
Page 34, Section 7 (Future Directions).

An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time?
title: Designing an FPTAS for stochastic probing and selection-stopping optimization problems
background_latex:
A (randomized) approximation scheme for an optimization problem takes an input instance of size \(n\) and an accuracy parameter \(\epsilon>0\), and outputs a feasible solution whose expected objective value is within a prescribed factor of optimal. An efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme (EPTAS) has running time \(t(\epsilon)\,\mathrm{poly}(n)\), where \(t(\epsilon)\) depends only on \(\epsilon\). A fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) has running time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\).

The paper studies several stochastic combinatorial optimization problems in which one is given independent nonnegative random variables \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) (specified by their distributions) and must choose either:
1) A non-adaptive subset \(S\subseteq[n]\) of size at most \(k\) to probe, aiming to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\max_{i\in S} X_i]\) (ProbeMax); or
2) A non-adaptive permutation \(\sigma\in S_n\) and a stopping time \(\tau\) (adapted to the observed outcomes along \(\sigma\)) to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[X_{\sigma(\tau)}]\) (Free-Order Prophets);
with further variants such as Pandora's Box with Commitment and adaptive probing policies.

The paper provides EPTASes for these problems. It also introduces an auxiliary feasibility problem (a multi-dimensional Santa Claus variant) for which it gives an EPTAS but argues that an FPTAS is impossible for that auxiliary problem due to strong NP-hardness (already for 3 machines via a reduction from 3-Partition).
problem_definition_latex: For one or more of the stochastic optimization problems studied in the paper (such as Free-Order Prophets, non-adaptive ProbeMax, adaptive ProbeMax, and Pandora's Box with Commitment), determine whether there exists an approximation scheme that, for every \(\epsilon>0\), computes a \((1-\epsilon)\)-approximate solution in time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\). Equivalently, determine whether these problems admit an FPTAS.
latex:
A (randomized) approximation scheme for an optimization problem takes an input instance of size \(n\) and an accuracy parameter \(\epsilon>0\), and outputs a feasible solution whose expected objective value is within a prescribed factor of optimal. An efficient polynomial-time approximation scheme (EPTAS) has running time \(t(\epsilon)\,\mathrm{poly}(n)\), where \(t(\epsilon)\) depends only on \(\epsilon\). A fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) has running time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\).

The paper studies several stochastic combinatorial optimization problems in which one is given independent nonnegative random variables \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) (specified by their distributions) and must choose either:
1) A non-adaptive subset \(S\subseteq[n]\) of size at most \(k\) to probe, aiming to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\max_{i\in S} X_i]\) (ProbeMax); or
2) A non-adaptive permutation \(\sigma\in S_n\) and a stopping time \(\tau\) (adapted to the observed outcomes along \(\sigma\)) to maximize \(\mathbb{E}[X_{\sigma(\tau)}]\) (Free-Order Prophets);
with further variants such as Pandora's Box with Commitment and adaptive probing policies.

The paper provides EPTASes for these problems. It also introduces an auxiliary feasibility problem (a multi-dimensional Santa Claus variant) for which it gives an EPTAS but argues that an FPTAS is impossible for that auxiliary problem due to strong NP-hardness (already for 3 machines via a reduction from 3-Partition).

For one or more of the stochastic optimization problems studied in the paper (such as Free-Order Prophets, non-adaptive ProbeMax, adaptive ProbeMax, and Pandora's Box with Commitment), determine whether there exists an approximation scheme that, for every \(\epsilon>0\), computes a \((1-\epsilon)\)-approximate solution in time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\). Equivalently, determine whether these problems admit an FPTAS.
open_problem_quote: An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is existential/complexity-theoretic (existence of an FPTAS) rather than a concrete inequality likely to be falsified by a finite instance. Automated instance search is unlikely to produce decisive evidence without additional conjectured structural properties or hardness assumptions.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear automatically checkable fitness function for candidate algorithms at scale: evaluating whether a proposed scheme is an FPTAS requires proofs of approximation and worst-case running time, not just empirical performance on sampled instances.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
